input_dict.current_prompt:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.

output.query: Red Hot Chili Peppers discography 1989 EMI "Sock-Cess is a 1989 compilation album" "released exclusively in the UK by EMI"

retrieved_titles: The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers
Under the Covers: Essential Red Hot Chili Peppers
retrieved_doc_snippets: Sock-Cess | Sock-Cess is a 1989 compilation album by Red Hot Chili Peppers and was released exclusively in the UK by EMI as a promotional only release. The album contains tracks from the band's first four studio albums.

The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers | The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers (also titled Greatest Hits on some versions) is a compilation album by the Red Hot Chili Peppers, first released in 1994, containing tracks from the band's time signed to the EMI America record label. " What Hits!? " has all these songs and 8 more. This compilation has been reprinted at least 4 times from 1994 to 2005 with different cover art.

The Abbey Road E.P. | The Abbey Road E.P. is an EP by The Red Hot Chili Peppers, released in the UK in May 1988 through EMI America as a way of introducing UK fans to the band's back catalog as they were touring the UK that year. Out of the five tracks included on this EP, four had already been previously released on the band's studio albums, although the band's first two albums The Red Hot Chili Peppers and Freaky Styley were not released in the UK until 1990, thus it provided a way of introduc ...[truncated]

The Red Hot Chili Peppers (album) | The Red Hot Chili Peppers is the debut studio album by American rock band Red Hot Chili Peppers, released on August 10, 1984 on EMI Records. The album was produced by Gang of Four guitarist Andy Gill, and is the only album to feature Jack Sherman on guitar. Sherman was fired by the band after the album release tour, replaced by founding member Hillel Slovak. " Get Up and Jump" was the only single released from the album, but a music video was made for the song ...[truncated]
Out in L.A. | Out in L.A. is a compilation of rare tracks by the Red Hot Chili Peppers released November 1, 1994 on EMI.

Under the Covers: Essential Red Hot Chili Peppers | Under the Covers: Essential Red Hot Chili Peppers is a compilation album released on March 31, 1998 by the Red Hot Chili Peppers. All of the songs featured are covers mainly from the band's early albums.

Positive Mental Octopus | Positive Mental Octopus is a music video compilation from the alternative rock band Red Hot Chili Peppers and was released in 1990 by EMI. The compilation was only released on VHS and is now out of print. It was superseded by the 1992 "What Hits!? " VHS and later DVD, which contained all the videos from this compilation and more not included in this release.
source_state.summary_2: The 1989 EMI release was Sock-Cess — a 1989 compilation album by Red Hot Chili Peppers released exclusively in the UK as a promotional-only EMI release (corroborated by passage [1]).

output.query: Red Hot Chili Peppers Mother's Milk 1989 EMI Good Time Boys
